meta.title: 8.3 API Gateways and BFF
meta.description: Backend-for-frontend pattern, API composition, request routing, and gateway responsibilities.

blocks.prompt: Your mobile app needs data from 5 microservices to render the home screen. Should the app make 5 API calls, or should something aggregate the data?

blocks.content:
## The BFF (Backend-for-Frontend) pattern

Create a dedicated backend for each frontend type. The BFF aggregates data from multiple microservices and returns exactly what the frontend needs.

```
Mobile App → Mobile BFF → UserService, OrderService, RecommendationService
Web App → Web BFF → UserService, OrderService, RecommendationService
Admin → Admin BFF → UserService, OrderService, AnalyticsService
```

**Why per-frontend:** Mobile needs minimal data (bandwidth), web needs richer data, admin needs different data entirely. One API cannot serve all optimally.

### API composition at the gateway

The gateway aggregates responses from multiple services into one:

```
GET /home-screen
  → parallel: GET /users/me, GET /orders/recent, GET /recommendations
  → merge responses
  → return single payload
```

**Keep composition simple.** The gateway should merge responses, not contain business logic. If composition gets complex, extract it into a dedicated BFF service.

### Gateway responsibilities (right vs wrong)

**Right:** Routing, authentication, rate limiting, SSL termination, request logging, simple response aggregation.

**Wrong:** Business logic, data transformation, database queries, complex workflow orchestration.

**Evolving approach:** Start with a single API gateway. When different frontends diverge significantly, introduce BFFs. This avoids premature complexity.

BFF creates dedicated backends for each frontend type, returning exactly the data each needs. API gateways handle routing and cross-cutting concerns. Keep gateways thin — business logic belongs in services, not gateways.

blocks.prompt: Module 8.4 covers service meshes — how sidecars handle networking concerns like mTLS, retries, and observability across all services.
